name: Data Structures and Algorithms
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency in Data Structures and Algorithms (3-5 years experience) should be able to recognise when a brute-force or wrong-shape implementation must be replaced with the right algorithmic primitive (graph traversal, trie, heap, dynamic programming) and apply it inside an existing module while pinning the new contract with rigorous correctness AND performance tests. They are fluent with foundational graph algorithms (BFS/DFS, Dijkstra with a priority queue, topological sort, simple cycle detection), trees and tries, heap-based top-k and scheduling, 1D/2D dynamic programming, sliding-window with FIFO queue + counts map, and big-O reasoning under realistic data sizes. They make engineering trade-offs (build cost vs query cost for a trie, heap-of-k vs full sort, recursion depth vs iterative BFS, greedy vs DP) and justify them with measurable tests on production-realistic inputs. They handle non-trivial edge cases (same-node path, unreachable destination, empty prefix, regional inputs that break greedy, expired events in a sliding window) and keep public function signatures stable when refactoring internals. They do NOT yet implement Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, A*, max-flow, segment trees from scratch, suffix arrays/automata, KMP from scratch, or bitmask DP — those are expert-level.
long_scope:
{"sections": [{"name": "Foundational Graph Algorithms", "scope_included": "- Implements BFS and DFS on adjacency lists for connectivity, reachability, and level-order problems
- Implements Dijkstra's shortest path using the language's priority queue primitive
- Implements topological sort on DAGs and detects simple cycles
- Handles non-trivial edge cases: same-node path, unreachable destination, disconnected components
- Reasons about V vs E and chooses adjacency list vs matrix appropriately
- Iterative BFS/DFS to avoid stack-depth issues on large graphs", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Trees and Tries", "scope_included": "- Implements a Trie for prefix-based autocomplete or lookup
- Stores precomputed top-k metadata at trie nodes when query latency must be independent of catalog size
- Performs BST traversals (in-order, level-order) and simple LCA on small trees
- Reasons about the trade-off between trie build cost and query cost
- Avoids unnecessary tree balancing complexity (red-black/AVL/B-trees are out of scope)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Heaps and Priority Queues", "scope_included": "- Implements top-k via min-heap of size k (heappushpop pattern) and explains why it beats full-sort
- Implements k-way merge using a heap of (value, source_iterator)
- Implements sliding-window medians using two heaps
- Schedules events using a priority queue keyed on next-fire time
- Reasons about heap operations being O(log k) and end-of-stream sort being O(k log k)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Dynamic Programming (Bounded)", "scope_included": "- Implements 1D bottom-up DP problems (coin change, longest increasing subsequence, classic knapsack)
- Implements small 2D bottom-up DP problems (edit distance, simple grid problems)
- Defines state, transition, and base case explicitly before coding
- Recognises when greedy is provably wrong and DP is required (e.g. regional coin denominations)
- Avoids recursion that blows the language runtime's call stack on realistic inputs
- Reasons about O(amount * len(denominations)) and similar product-of-state-space complexities
- Does NOT use bitmask DP, multi-dimensional DP beyond 2D, or DP-on-trees (those are expert)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Hashing and Sliding Window", "scope_included": "- Maintains rolling-window aggregates with a FIFO queue/deque + counts map
- Removes expired entries from the window in the same pass that adds new ones (O(n) amortised)
- Handles multiple matches in the same hash bucket without producing duplicate pairs (e.g. n*(n-1)/2 vs n)
- Recognises anagram-window and longest-substring-without-repeats patterns", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Algorithm-Aware Refactoring Inside a Real Module", "scope_included": "- Replaces O(n^2) inner loops with O(n) hash-grouped passes when keys are stable
- Replaces full sort with heap-of-k when k << n
- Replaces recursive DFS with iterative BFS when stack depth is a concern
- Replaces greedy with DP when greedy is provably wrong
- Keeps public function signatures stable while changing internals
- Adds the minimum surface area to the codebase", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Big-O Reasoning Under Realistic Data Sizes", "scope_included": "- Justifies complexity in terms of V, E, n, k, prefix_length, amount, etc.
- Picks performance-test input sizes that distinguish the new bound from the old (1000 nodes <100ms; 200K events <5ms per call)
- Avoids over-claiming complexity (e.g. claiming O(log n) when worst-case is O(n))
- Reasons about amortised vs worst-case bounds", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Tests for Intermediate DSA Code", "scope_included": "- Writes correctness tests on hand-built fixtures including the bug regression case
- Writes a performance test on production-realistic input sizes that pins the new complexity contract
- Pins edge cases (same-node path, unreachable, empty prefix, three-way matches, expired events, regional denominations)
- Keeps tests deterministic with seeded randomness when needed", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Quality, Readability, and Working in a Codebase", "scope_included": "- Writes idiomatic code in the project's primary language
- Keeps function signatures stable when refactoring internals
- Uses standard library and built-in primitives only (no specialised third-party DSA libraries unless justified)
- Avoids premature abstractions and unused helpers", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "Out-of-Scope (NOT expected at INTERMEDIATE)", "scope_included": "- Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, A*, max-flow, min-cut
- Segment trees, Fenwick trees, suffix arrays/automata implemented from scratch
- KMP, Aho-Corasick implemented from scratch
- Bitmask DP, multi-dimensional DP beyond 2D, DP-on-trees
- Advanced approximation algorithms or NP-hard reductions
- Custom balanced BST implementations (red-black, AVL, B-trees)", "is_non_core_section": true}]}
